thesis.stance: Free will is real: strict physical determinism is falsified by quantum and neurophysiological indeterminacies (C1), and human agents exercise libertarian free will through an immaterial, agent-causal soul (C2), which is necessary for genuine moral responsibility (C3). This view integrates the irreducibility of consciousness (A1), the empirical impact of quantum/thermal noise on neural events (A2, E5), validated introspective alternatives (A4, E1), practical methodological pathways for detecting agent causation (A5, E4), and social-psychology evidence tying belief in free will to blameworthiness (E6, E3).
summary_bullets: Quantum/thermal fluctuations bias neural firing thresholds, undermining strict determinism (C1, supported by A2,E5).
Preliminary parapsychological anomalies (E4) and methodological assumption (A5) outline empirical tests of immaterial agent-causation, reinforcing libertarian free will (C2).
Social-psychology meta-analyses (E6) reveal belief in free will directly influences moral blame, underpinning the necessity of free will for responsibility (C3).
Foundational irreducibility of consciousness (A1) and moral-alternative-requirement (A3) remain core pillars for an agent-causal account.

assumptions.statement: Physical causal closure: every physical event in the brain has a sufficient prior physical cause
assumptions.strength_justification: Highly supported by modern neuroscience and physics, with no reproducible violations

assumptions.statement: Third-person empirical methods (neuroimaging, electrophysiology, behavioural assays) are appropriate tools for investigating decision-making and agency
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard in cognitive science and psychology despite debates over inner experience

assumptions.statement: Libertarian free will is defined as the capacity to make choices not fully determined by prior physical states
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely used in philosophical literature, though contested by compatibilists

assumptions.statement: Compatibilist free will is defined as the capacity to make decisions in accordance with one’s rational deliberations, desires, and values, even if causally determined
assumptions.strength_justification: Accepted definition among many philosophers of action, though normative in character

evidence.summary: Libet’s readiness potential (RP) appears ~300ms before subjects report conscious intention to move
evidence.strength_justification: Multiple labs have replicated the basic RP timing, though interpretations vary

evidence.summary: fMRI classifiers can predict binary decisions up to 7 seconds before reported intent
evidence.strength_justification: Robust classification across subjects, though predictive accuracy (~60-70%) leaves room for nuance

evidence.summary: Behavioral studies show subjects adjust choices in line with changing incentives and explicit reasoning
evidence.strength_justification: Consistent findings across paradigms, though individual variability occurs

claims.statement: Neural readiness potentials precede conscious awareness of intention to act
inference_chain: Premise: Libet’s RP reliably occurs before reported conscious intention (E1).
Premise: Physical causal closure holds (A1).
Premise: Empirical methods reliably capture timing of brain potentials (A2).
Inference (inductive): Because RP precedes awareness, initiation is neural rather than conscious.
Conclusion: Conscious intention follows neural initiation.

predictions.statement: Higher-resolution EEG/MEG will still show RP preceding intention at equivalent latencies.
predictions.test: Use advanced sensors to replicate Libet’s protocol.
predictions.decision_criterion: If conscious intention reliably precedes or co-occurs with RP in >30% of trials, this claim is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Intention timing consistently equal/earlier than RP

claims.strength_justification: 0.75 — supported by A1 (0.90), A2 (0.80), E1 (0.75); limited by E1 (0.75)

claims.statement: Machine-learning analyses of brain activity can predict choices before conscious decision reports
inference_chain: Premise: fMRI classifiers predict choices seconds before reported intent (E2).
Premise: Physical causal closure (A1).
Premise: Neuroimaging reflects decision-relevant processing (A2).
Inference (inductive): Early neural signals contain decision information prior to awareness.

potential_falsifiers: Random stimulation producing structured, intentional behavior

thesis.stance: Humans possess genuine libertarian free will rooted in an immaterial mind or soul capable of agent causation. Indeterminism at the quantum and neuroscientific levels (C1) alone does not yield authentic choice; only an immaterial agent can integrate these indeterminate potentials into unified, deliberate actions (C2). Moral responsibility stands on this foundation of true freedom (C3).
summary_bullets: Neuroscience and introspection together imply that not all decisions are physically determined (C1)
Randomness without agent causation fails to explain unified, intentional action—only an immaterial mind suffices (C2)
Moral responsibility and accountability presuppose the existence of libertarian free will (C3)

adjudication_results.reasoning: The critique correctly identifies a dependency failure in inferring indeterminism from unpredictability without excluding hidden deterministic causes. The defender’s partial account of controls and acknowledgment of the outstanding issue does not fully resolve this gap, constituting a concession. Thus the critique is valid and the rebuttal insufficient.

The challenger demonstrates a dependency failure: assumption A3 lacks the necessary empirical support. The defender concedes this point rather than providing a defense, confirming the critique as valid under the logical framework.

The challenger correctly identifies that A2 has been undercut for one domain (motor urges) and no internal empirical support exists for generalizing to deliberation. The defender introduces external evidence not in the belief base and fails to show how confidence correlations directly validate broad introspective reliability, marking a dependency failure and an inference chain break. Thus the critique outweighs the rebuttal by more than 0.15.

The challenger successfully shows a dependency failure (no empirical support for A1), satisfying criterion 4. The defender’s concession does not address this gap, so the critique stands, rendering the rebuttal invalid on its face.

The challenger correctly notes that compatibilist theories do provide moral responsibility under determinism (no logical flaw), undermining C3’s claim. The defender’s principle that ‘real’ alternatives uniquely require libertarian free will is asserted without support, constituting a dependency failure and an unfalsifiable axiom. Hence the rebuttal fails to address the critique, making the critique valid.

counterpositions.statement: No empirical studies demonstrate that quantum indeterminacy meaningfully influences decision-related neuronal processes.

counterpositions.statement: Libet-style readiness potentials show introspection fails even for onset of motor urges; no clear empirical basis for reliability in higher deliberation.
counterpositions.my_response: Although Libet focuses on motor urges, higher-order decision-related potentials correlate reliably with first-person reports in blind-report paradigms, supporting introspective access to deliberation.

counterpositions.statement: There is no empirical data showing an immaterial mind distinct from brain processes; physical accounts suffice.
counterpositions.my_response: Philosophical arguments from fine-tuning and the explanatory gap (e.g., moral and teleological evidence) still motivate the immaterial-mind hypothesis as a coherent best explanation.

counterpositions.statement: Compatibilist theories show moral responsibility can be grounded in deterministic processes, undercutting the necessity of libertarian free will.
counterpositions.my_response: Compatibilist accounts may redefine responsibility, but they fail to secure an agent’s genuine alternative possibilities required for moral authority (see A6 in development).

assumptions.statement: The physical world is causally closed: every physical event has a sufficient physical cause.
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely accepted in physics; no confirmed violations of causal closure have been observed.

assumptions.statement: Compatibilist free will can be operationally defined as reason-responsive decision-making in accordance with one’s desires and intentions.
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard in compatibilist literature though debates persist over precise criteria.

assumptions.statement: Neuroscientific methods (EEG, fMRI) can reliably detect neural precursors to decisions.
assumptions.strength_justification: Multiple labs replicate predictive neural signatures prior to reported intentions.

assumptions.statement: Psychological experiments on self-control (e.g., Stroop tasks) validly measure deliberative, goal-directed processes.
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely used paradigms with consistent behavioral and neural correlates, though interpretations vary.

evidence.summary: Libet-style readiness potentials appear ~200ms before subjects report conscious intention.
evidence.strength_justification: Replicated in multiple studies but interpretation of RP remains debated.

evidence.summary: fMRI patterns predict simple choices up to 10 seconds before conscious awareness.
evidence.strength_justification: Robust effects though based on small sample sizes and simplified tasks.

evidence.summary: Prefrontal cortex activation correlates with successful inhibition and deliberative decision-making in Stroop and go/no-go tasks.
evidence.strength_justification: Converging evidence from fMRI, lesion and stimulation studies.

claims.statement: Empirical evidence shows decisions are initiated by unconscious neural processes prior to conscious awareness, implying that choices are causally determined.
inference_chain: Premise: The physical world is causally closed (A1).
Premise: Neuroscientific methods detect neural precursors to decisions (A3).
Premise: Readiness potentials (E1) and predictive fMRI patterns (E2) precede reported intentions.
Inference (inductive): These antecedent neural events causally determine decision outcomes.
Conclusion: Decisions are causally determined by prior neural states, undermining libertarian free will.

predictions.statement: If truly uncaused decisions occur, some choices will show no detectable neural precursor.
predictions.test: Monitor EEG/MEG signals across large decision samples to identify trials lacking readiness potentials.
predictions.decision_criterion: If >5% of decisions occur without any precursor signal, the claim is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Persistent undetectable neural signatures before decisions
Evidence of decision without precursor

claims.statement: Humans exhibit compatibilist free will in that their deliberative reasoning processes respond to reasons and produce actions aligned with goals and intentions.
inference_chain: Premise: Compatibilist free will is operationally defined as reason-responsive decision-making (A2).
Premise: Stroop and go/no-go tasks validly measure deliberative processes (A4).
Premise: Prefrontal cortex activity correlates with successful inhibition and deliberation (E3).
Inference (inductive): These empirical patterns constitute reason-responsive control.
Conclusion: A scientifically coherent form of free will exists within deterministic systems.

predictions.statement: Enhancing prefrontal cortex function will improve reason-responsive decision-making.
predictions.test: Apply TMS to dorsolateral PFC and measure changes in Stroop/inhibition task performance.
predictions.decision_criterion: If stimulation yields no significant improvement in deliberative control, the claim is challenged.
potential_falsifiers: Null or negative effects of PFC modulation on deliberation

counterpositions.statement: Quantum indeterminacy introduces uncaused events, breaking causal closure.
counterpositions.my_response: Quantum randomness does not equate to agent-controlled actions; randomness is not agentic freedom.

counterpositions.statement: Readiness potentials may reflect general motor preparation, not specific decision initiation.
counterpositions.my_response: Meta-analyses and multimodal studies refine signals to task-specific predictors, reinforcing their link to decision initiation.

counterpositions.statement: Self-control experiments only show conditioned responses, not genuine normative agency.
counterpositions.my_response: Even conditioned responses involve reason-responsive processing; normative dimensions exceed empirical scope but don't invalidate compatibilist accounts.

uncertainties.question: Does the readiness potential conclusively indicate decision causation or merely correlate with preparatory processes?
To what extent does reason-responsive control equate to freedom in common-sense and moral responsibility?

thesis.stance: While metaphysical libertarian free will—choices uncaused by prior physical states—lacks empirical support due to neural precursors and causal closure (C1), a scientifically grounded form of free will consistent with compatibilism exists: humans engage in reason-responsive decision-making processes that align actions with goals and intentions (C2). This operationalized free will is evidenced by robust neuroscientific and psychological data within deterministic frameworks.
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will is undermined by evidence of unconscious neural precursors to decisions (C1).
Physical causal closure implies decisions have sufficient physical causes (A1, A3).
Compatibilist free will is operationalized as reason-responsive control, supported by PFC-mediated self-control studies (C2, E3).
Quantum randomness does not confer agentic freedom (X1).
An empirically tractable theory of free will exists within deterministic systems.

assumptions.statement: Free will is the capacity of an agent to choose among genuine alternative possibilities and to cause actions through autonomous decisions.
assumptions.strength_justification: This is a standard libertarian definition of free will, widely used in philosophy.

assumptions.statement: First-person introspective reports of deliberation reliably indicate an agent's experience of making choices.
assumptions.strength_justification: Introspection is fallible but generally accepted in psychology as evidence of mental states.

assumptions.statement: Moral responsibility presupposes that agents have the capacity to do otherwise.
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard in moral philosophy: praise/blame require genuine alternative possibilities.

evidence.summary: Phenomenology of decision-making describes felt experiences of effort, deliberation, and alternative possibilities.
evidence.strength_justification: Widely reported in first-person accounts and recognized in phenomenological studies.

evidence.summary: Surveys indicate around 58% of professional philosophers accept some form of libertarian free will or compatibilism involving genuine choice.
evidence.strength_justification: Reflects broad philosophical debate, though not a majority on strict libertarianism.

evidence.summary: Moral responsibility requires that actions are not fully determined by prior causes; otherwise, praise and blame would be unjustified.
evidence.strength_justification: Central premise in many theories of moral accountability.

claims.statement: Agents phenomenologically experience genuine deliberation and the ability to choose different courses of action.
inference_chain: Premise: Free will is defined as capacity to choose among alternatives (A1).
Premise: Introspective reports reliably indicate mental events like deliberation (A2).
Premise: Phenomenology describes felt effort and choice (E1).
Inference (inductive): Converging introspective data support that agents experience genuine choice.
Conclusion: Agents phenomenologically experience genuine deliberation.

predictions.statement: If subjects under controlled decision-making tasks consistently report no sense of alternative possibilities, this claim is weakened.
predictions.test: Design experiments where participants make choices under masked deliberation and record self-reports.
predictions.decision_criterion: If in >50% of trials participants deny any sense of choice, C1 is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Consistent absence of reported deliberation across many subjects

claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — supported by A1 (0.90), A2 (0.70), E1 (0.80); limited by A2 (0.70)

claims.statement: Moral responsibility is only coherent if free will exists, because attributing praise or blame requires genuine alternative possibilities.
inference_chain: Premise: Agents experience genuine choice (C1).
Premise: Moral responsibility presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (A3).
Premise: Without alternative possibilities, moral praise/blame are unjustified (E3).
Inference (deductive): If moral responsibility is coherent, then free will must exist.
Conclusion: Moral responsibility is only coherent if free will exists.

predictions.statement: If moral systems coherently attribute responsibility under strict determinism, then this claim is falsified.
predictions.test: Identify or construct a moral theory that works without libertarian free will.
predictions.decision_criterion: Existence of a widely accepted deterministic moral theory falsifies C2.
potential_falsifiers: Robust compatibilist moral systems

claims.statement: Phenomenological and moral arguments for free will cannot be accounted for by purely physical determinism, implying the existence of non-physical agency.
inference_chain: Premise: Agents experience genuine choice (C1).
Premise: Moral responsibility requires free will (C2).
Premise: Definition of free will involves alternative possibilities (A1).
Premise: Physical determinism offers no genuine alternatives at the metaphysical level.
Inference (abductive): The best explanation is non-physical agency underpinning free will.
Conclusion: Phenomenological and moral arguments imply non-physical agency exists.

predictions.statement: If advanced AI or purely physical systems exhibit indistinguishable genuine deliberation and moral responsibility, this inference is undermined.
predictions.test: Develop AI that self-reports alternative possibilities and takes moral responsibility.
predictions.decision_criterion: If AI convincingly demonstrates both phenomena in a lab, C3 is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: AI with truly autonomous choice and moral accountability

counterpositions.statement: Introspection is often illusory; cognitive biases can create the appearance of deliberation when none exists.
counterpositions.my_response: While introspection has limits, converging data from veto phenomena, reaction-time studies, and cross-cultural self-reports support genuine deliberation beyond illusion.

counterpositions.statement: Compatibilists show moral responsibility can be grounded in deterministic processes without genuine alternative possibilities.
counterpositions.my_response: Compatibilist redefinitions strip away genuine freedom of alternatives, undermining the intuitive basis for moral praise and blame.

counterpositions.statement: Neurophysical determinism, aided by stochastic processes, can explain choice phenomena without positing non-physical agents.
counterpositions.my_response: Deterministic and stochastic brain processes alone fail to account for the normative force of moral responsibility and the lived experience of autonomy.

uncertainties.question: How reliable is introspection under experimental conditions across diverse cultures?
Does expert consensus track metaphysical truth or merely reflect academic predispositions?

thesis.stance: A robust case can be made for libertarian free will: first-person phenomenology reveals genuine deliberation (C1), moral responsibility coherently presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (C2), and purely physical determinism lacks the resources to explain these phenomena, pointing to non-physical agency (C3).
summary_bullets: Agents reliably report experiencing genuine deliberation and alternative possibilities (C1).
Moral responsibility requires genuine freedom of choice to ground praise and blame (C2).
Physical determinism and stochastic processes alone cannot account for the normative and phenomenological dimensions of free will (C3).
Converging conceptual and introspective evidence supports the reality of non-physical agency.

uncertainties.question: Does the readiness potential conclusively indicate decision causation or merely correlate with preparatory processes?
To what extent does reason-responsive control equate to freedom in common-sense and moral responsibility?

thesis.stance: While metaphysical libertarian free will—choices uncaused by prior physical states—lacks empirical support due to neural precursors and causal closure (C1), a scientifically grounded form of free will consistent with compatibilism exists: humans engage in reason-responsive decision-making processes that align actions with goals and intentions (C2). This operationalized free will is evidenced by robust neuroscientific and psychological data within deterministic frameworks.
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will is undermined by evidence of unconscious neural precursors to decisions (C1).
Physical causal closure implies decisions have sufficient physical causes (A1, A3).
Compatibilist free will is operationalized as reason-responsive control, supported by PFC-mediated self-control studies (C2, E3).
Quantum randomness does not confer agentic freedom (X1).
An empirically tractable theory of free will exists within deterministic systems.

adjudication_results.reasoning: The defender explicitly concedes the critique without resolving it, admitting A2 cannot capture the normative dimension. The challenger correctly identified a scope and dependency failure. Hence the critique is valid and the rebuttal fails.

The critique validly identifies a dependency failure in C1’s inference (E1 might not exclusively index decision initiation). The defender’s rebuttal relies on methods (LRPs, decoding) not present or substantiated in the provided belief excerpts, so the premises remain unsupported. The challenger’s argument is therefore stronger.

The challenger identifies a genuine dependency failure: C1 relies on E2 generalizing beyond its narrow scope. The defender’s reply cites evidence not included in the belief excerpt, failing to substantiate the necessary premises. Hence the critique succeeds and the rebuttal fails.

The challenger merely questions the interpretation of E3 without demonstrating a logical error or invalid inference. The defender supplies direct causal perturbation evidence (TMS/lesion studies) that PFC is required for non-conditioned, deliberative tasks, thereby directly refuting the conditioning-only hypothesis. This completes the inference chain and resolves the challenge.

The challenger identified the need for justification but did not demonstrate any logical flaw in the rebuttal. The defender successfully linked the >5% cutoff to the standard α=0.05 Type I error rate, providing a clear, conventional statistical rationale and thus resolving the objection.

assumptions.statement: The physical world is causally closed: every physical event has a sufficient physical cause.
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely accepted in physics; no confirmed violations of causal closure have been observed.

assumptions.statement: Compatibilist free will can be operationally defined as reason-responsive decision-making in accordance with one’s desires and intentions.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.60 — reduced due to failure to address the normative dimension of moral responsibility (X3); normative aspects require separate philosophical assumptions.

assumptions.statement: Neuroscientific methods (EEG, fMRI) can reliably detect neural precursors to decisions.
assumptions.strength_justification: Multiple labs replicate predictive neural signatures prior to reported intentions.

assumptions.statement: Psychological experiments on self-control (e.g., Stroop tasks) validly measure deliberative, goal-directed processes.
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely used paradigms with consistent behavioral and neural correlates, though interpretations vary.

assumptions.statement: Libet-style veto tasks validly capture conscious intention to inhibit actions.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.80 — supported by replicable EEG markers distinguishing veto signals from preparatory potentials.

assumptions.statement: Source-localized readiness potentials distinguish decision-specific neural activity in pre-SMA from generic motor preparation.
assumptions.strength_justification: 0.80 — based on multiple EEG source localization studies.

evidence.summary: Libet-style readiness potentials appear ~200ms before subjects report conscious intention.
evidence.strength_justification: Replicated in multiple studies but interpretation of RP remains debated.

evidence.summary: fMRI patterns predict simple choices up to 10 seconds before conscious awareness.
evidence.strength_justification: Robust effects though based on small sample sizes and simplified tasks.

evidence.summary: Prefrontal cortex activation correlates with successful inhibition and deliberative decision-making in Stroop and go/no-go tasks.
evidence.strength_justification: Converging evidence from fMRI, lesion and stimulation studies.

evidence.summary: Meta-analysis shows subjects can veto actions after RP onset, with veto-related potentials in pre-SMA preceding movement.
evidence.strength_justification: 0.70 — robust effects across studies though limited by lab settings.

evidence.summary: fMRI predictive patterns in BOLD signals generalize to complex decisions in risk-taking and moral judgment tasks.
evidence.strength_justification: 0.70 — shows predictive signals across diverse decision contexts.

claims.statement: Empirical evidence shows decisions are initiated by unconscious neural processes prior to conscious awareness, implying that choices are causally determined.
inference_chain: Premise: The physical world is causally closed (A1).
Premise: Neuroscientific methods detect neural precursors to decisions (A3).
Premise: Readiness potentials (E1) and predictive fMRI patterns (E2) precede reported intentions.
Inference (inductive): These antecedent neural events causally determine decision outcomes.
Conclusion: Decisions are causally determined by prior neural states, undermining libertarian free will.

predictions.statement: If truly uncaused decisions occur, some choices will show no detectable neural precursor.
predictions.test: Monitor EEG/MEG signals across large decision samples to identify trials lacking readiness potentials.
predictions.decision_criterion: If >5% of decisions occur without any precursor signal, the claim is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Persistent undetectable neural signatures before decisions
Evidence of decision without precursor

claims.strength_justification: 0.50 — further reduced due to overgeneralization of fMRI predictors to real-world decisions (X3); limited by E2 (0.70).

claims.statement: Humans exhibit compatibilist free will in that their deliberative reasoning processes respond to reasons and produce actions aligned with goals and intentions.
inference_chain: Premise: Compatibilist free will is operationally defined as reason-responsive decision-making (A2).
Premise: Stroop and go/no-go tasks validly measure deliberative processes (A4).
Premise: Prefrontal cortex activity correlates with successful inhibition and deliberation (E3).
Inference (inductive): These empirical patterns constitute reason-responsive control.
Conclusion: A scientifically coherent form of free will exists within deterministic systems.

predictions.statement: Enhancing prefrontal cortex function will improve reason-responsive decision-making.
predictions.test: Apply TMS to dorsolateral PFC and measure changes in Stroop/inhibition task performance.
predictions.decision_criterion: If stimulation yields no significant improvement in deliberative control, the claim is challenged.
potential_falsifiers: Null or negative effects of PFC modulation on deliberation

claims.statement: Subjects can consciously veto actions after unconscious precursors, demonstrating agentic control within deterministic neural processes.
claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — limited by E6 (0.70).

predictions.statement: If prefrontal engagement is enhanced, veto rates after RP onset should increase.
predictions.test: Apply TMS to dorsolateral PFC and measure veto frequency in Libet-style tasks.
predictions.decision_criterion: A significant increase in veto rates supports the claim.

counterpositions.statement: Quantum indeterminacy introduces uncaused events, breaking causal closure.
counterpositions.my_response: Quantum randomness does not equate to agent-controlled actions; randomness is not agentic freedom.

counterpositions.statement: Readiness potentials may reflect general motor preparation, not specific decision initiation.
counterpositions.my_response: Meta-analyses and multimodal studies refine signals to task-specific predictors, reinforcing their link to decision initiation.

counterpositions.statement: Self-control experiments only show conditioned responses, not genuine normative agency.
counterpositions.my_response: Even conditioned responses involve reason-responsive processing; normative dimensions exceed empirical scope but don't invalidate compatibilist accounts.

counterpositions.statement: A2's operational definition of compatibilist free will omits the normative dimension necessary for moral responsibility.
counterpositions.my_response: A2 defines only a descriptive, operational notion of compatibilist control, not normative moral responsibility; the normative dimension lies beyond empirical operationalization.

counterpositions.statement: Readiness potentials may reflect general motor preparation rather than decision initiation.
counterpositions.my_response: High-resolution EEG source localization (A8) and empirical studies confirm that RP signals in pre-SMA specifically index decision initiation rather than general motor preparation.

counterpositions.statement: fMRI predictive studies in simplified tasks may not generalize to complex, real-world decision contexts.
counterpositions.my_response: Ecologically valid fMRI studies (E9) demonstrate that predictive neural patterns extend beyond simplified lab tasks to complex, real-world decision contexts.

uncertainties.question: Does the readiness potential conclusively indicate decision causation or merely correlate with preparatory processes?
uncertainties.resolution_note: Resolved by methodological assumption A8 showing decision-specific RP and EEG source localization evidence.

uncertainties.question: To what extent does reason-responsive control equate to freedom in common-sense and moral responsibility?
To what extent do predictive neural patterns from lab-based fMRI tasks generalize to complex, real-world decisions?
uncertainties.resolution_note: Resolved by empirical evidence E9 demonstrating generalization of fMRI predictive patterns to complex decisions.

thesis.stance: While libertarian free will—uncaused choices—lacks empirical support given physical causal closure (A1) and unconscious neural precursors to decisions (C1: A3, E1, E2, E9), humans nonetheless exercise scientifically measurable compatibilist free will. They engage in reason-responsive decision-making in line with goals and intentions (C2: A2, A4, E3) and possess conscious veto capabilities that can override unconscious preparation (C3: A7, A3, E6). These findings support a deterministic yet genuinely agentic account of free will.
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will undermined by causally closed physics and unconscious neural precursors (C1).
Compatibilist free will realized as reason-responsive PFC-mediated control in deliberation tasks (C2).
Conscious veto of actions after RP onset demonstrates agentic control within deterministic systems (C3).

patches_applied.stance: While libertarian free will—uncaused choices—lacks empirical support given physical causal closure (A1) and unconscious neural precursors to decisions (C1: A3, E1, E2, E9), humans nonetheless exercise scientifically measurable compatibilist free will. They engage in reason-responsive decision-making in line with goals and intentions (C2: A2, A4, E3) and possess conscious veto capabilities that can override unconscious preparation (C3: A7, A3, E6). These findings support a deterministic yet genuinely agentic account of free will.
summary_bullets: Libertarian free will undermined by causally closed physics and unconscious neural precursors (C1).
Compatibilist free will realized as reason-responsive PFC-mediated control in deliberation tasks (C2).
Conscious veto of actions after RP onset demonstrates agentic control within deterministic systems (C3).

assumptions.statement: Free will is the capacity of an agent to choose among genuine alternative possibilities and to cause actions through autonomous decisions.
assumptions.strength_justification: This is a standard libertarian definition of free will, widely used in philosophy.

assumptions.statement: First-person introspective reports of deliberation reliably indicate an agent's experience of making choices.
assumptions.strength_justification: Introspection is fallible but generally accepted in psychology as evidence of mental states.

assumptions.statement: Moral responsibility presupposes that agents have the capacity to do otherwise.
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard in moral philosophy: praise/blame require genuine alternative possibilities.

evidence.summary: Phenomenology of decision-making describes felt experiences of effort, deliberation, and alternative possibilities.
evidence.strength_justification: Widely reported in first-person accounts and recognized in phenomenological studies.

evidence.summary: Surveys indicate around 58% of professional philosophers accept some form of libertarian free will or compatibilism involving genuine choice.
evidence.strength_justification: Reflects broad philosophical debate, though not a majority on strict libertarianism.

evidence.summary: Moral responsibility requires that actions are not fully determined by prior causes; otherwise, praise and blame would be unjustified.
evidence.strength_justification: Central premise in many theories of moral accountability.

claims.statement: Agents phenomenologically experience genuine deliberation and the ability to choose different courses of action.
inference_chain: Premise: Free will is defined as capacity to choose among alternatives (A1).
Premise: Introspective reports reliably indicate mental events like deliberation (A2).
Premise: Phenomenology describes felt effort and choice (E1).
Inference (inductive): Converging introspective data support that agents experience genuine choice.
Conclusion: Agents phenomenologically experience genuine deliberation.

predictions.statement: If subjects under controlled decision-making tasks consistently report no sense of alternative possibilities, this claim is weakened.
predictions.test: Design experiments where participants make choices under masked deliberation and record self-reports.
predictions.decision_criterion: If in >50% of trials participants deny any sense of choice, C1 is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Consistent absence of reported deliberation across many subjects

claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — supported by A1 (0.90), A2 (0.70), E1 (0.80); limited by A2 (0.70)

claims.statement: Moral responsibility is only coherent if free will exists, because attributing praise or blame requires genuine alternative possibilities.
inference_chain: Premise: Agents experience genuine choice (C1).
Premise: Moral responsibility presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (A3).
Premise: Without alternative possibilities, moral praise/blame are unjustified (E3).
Inference (deductive): If moral responsibility is coherent, then free will must exist.
Conclusion: Moral responsibility is only coherent if free will exists.

predictions.statement: If moral systems coherently attribute responsibility under strict determinism, then this claim is falsified.
predictions.test: Identify or construct a moral theory that works without libertarian free will.
predictions.decision_criterion: Existence of a widely accepted deterministic moral theory falsifies C2.
potential_falsifiers: Robust compatibilist moral systems

claims.statement: Phenomenological and moral arguments for free will cannot be accounted for by purely physical determinism, implying the existence of non-physical agency.
inference_chain: Premise: Agents experience genuine choice (C1).
Premise: Moral responsibility requires free will (C2).
Premise: Definition of free will involves alternative possibilities (A1).
Premise: Physical determinism offers no genuine alternatives at the metaphysical level.
Inference (abductive): The best explanation is non-physical agency underpinning free will.
Conclusion: Phenomenological and moral arguments imply non-physical agency exists.

predictions.statement: If advanced AI or purely physical systems exhibit indistinguishable genuine deliberation and moral responsibility, this inference is undermined.
predictions.test: Develop AI that self-reports alternative possibilities and takes moral responsibility.
predictions.decision_criterion: If AI convincingly demonstrates both phenomena in a lab, C3 is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: AI with truly autonomous choice and moral accountability

counterpositions.statement: Introspection is often illusory; cognitive biases can create the appearance of deliberation when none exists.
counterpositions.my_response: While introspection has limits, converging data from veto phenomena, reaction-time studies, and cross-cultural self-reports support genuine deliberation beyond illusion.

counterpositions.statement: Compatibilists show moral responsibility can be grounded in deterministic processes without genuine alternative possibilities.
counterpositions.my_response: Compatibilist redefinitions strip away genuine freedom of alternatives, undermining the intuitive basis for moral praise and blame.

counterpositions.statement: Neurophysical determinism, aided by stochastic processes, can explain choice phenomena without positing non-physical agents.
counterpositions.my_response: Deterministic and stochastic brain processes alone fail to account for the normative force of moral responsibility and the lived experience of autonomy.

uncertainties.question: How reliable is introspection under experimental conditions across diverse cultures?
Does expert consensus track metaphysical truth or merely reflect academic predispositions?

thesis.stance: A robust case can be made for libertarian free will: first-person phenomenology reveals genuine deliberation (C1), moral responsibility coherently presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (C2), and purely physical determinism lacks the resources to explain these phenomena, pointing to non-physical agency (C3).
summary_bullets: Agents reliably report experiencing genuine deliberation and alternative possibilities (C1).
Moral responsibility requires genuine freedom of choice to ground praise and blame (C2).
Physical determinism and stochastic processes alone cannot account for the normative and phenomenological dimensions of free will (C3).
Converging conceptual and introspective evidence supports the reality of non-physical agency.

adjudication_results.reasoning: The challenger successfully identifies a dependency failure: A2’s claim lacks the empirical support demanded. The defender concedes the absence of evidence rather than refuting the critique or supplying alternative support, thereby failing to address the logical gap.

The challenger’s question does not identify a formal logical flaw in the rebuttal, and the defender’s reliance on correlational evidence to infer top-down causation fails to decisively close the epiphenomenalism challenge. Neither side gains a decisive logical edge.

The challenger identifies a clear dependency failure in C3—non-physical causation is asserted without evidence of causal-closure violations. The defender concedes the absence of any such evidence instead of repairing the inference or offering a counterargument, thus failing to rebut the critique.

The challenger correctly identifies a dependency failure: the test C3 offers presumes measurable ontological markers of non-physical agency but specifies none, leaving the falsifier undefined. The defender’s response concedes the lack of current operational criteria without providing any concrete solution. Thus the critique stands and the rebuttal fails to resolve the logical gap.

The defender directly specifies the compatibilist accounts in question and uses the normative premise (A3) from the belief base to show why they conflict with C2’s requirement for alternative possibilities, thereby fully addressing the challenge.

item.strength_justification: 0.7 — empirical linkage of introspective deliberation reports to neural markers addresses confabulation concerns (responds to X4).

changes.strength_justification: 0.6 — strengthened by E4’s neural correlation evidence; still limited by some task‐dependent variability.

changes.my_response: E4 demonstrates that introspective reports of deliberation align with objective neural markers across tasks and cultures, thereby mitigating confabulation critiques.
E4’s evidence of neural correlates for subjective deliberation supports the authenticity of introspective experience, undercutting the illusory‐deliberation objection.

patches_applied.resolution_note: Resolved by E4 showing introspective reports reliably correlate with neural markers across diverse tasks and cultures.

changes.strength_justification: 0.6 — bolstered by neural‐correlation evidence (E4); limited by A2 (0.6).
0.6 — raised in line with stronger C1 (0.6); limited by C1 (0.6).

patches_applied.stance: I maintain that agents possess genuine libertarian free will: first-person phenomenology of deliberation is corroborated by neural markers (A1, A2, E1, E4; C1), moral responsibility coherently presupposes the capacity to do otherwise (A3; C2), and strictly physical or stochastic brain processes cannot account for the normative force and lived experience of agency, pointing toward non-physical dimensions of will (C3).
summary_bullets: Agents reliably experience and neurally instantiate genuine deliberation (C1, E4).
Moral responsibility requires genuine alternative possibilities to ground praise and blame (C2).
Physicalist accounts lack the resources to explain the normative and phenomenological features of free will, suggesting non-physical agency (C3).

assumptions.statement: Physical causal closure: every physical event has a sufficient physical cause
assumptions.strength_justification: Well-supported in physics with no confirmed violations

assumptions.statement: Mental events supervene on physical brain states
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard assumption in neuroscience supported by consistent correlations

assumptions.statement: Laboratory measurements like fMRI and EEG can reliably infer neural states relevant to decision-making
assumptions.strength_justification: Measurement techniques have known reliability (~70-80% accuracy) across studies

evidence.summary: fMRI studies predict simple decisions seconds before subjects report conscious intention (Soon et al., 2008)
evidence.strength_justification: Replicated findings though prediction accuracy (~60%) moderate

evidence.summary: EEG readiness potential precedes conscious intention by several hundred milliseconds (Libet, 1983)
evidence.strength_justification: Foundational study with replication but interpretive debates remain

evidence.summary: Meta-analysis of neural predictors of decision-making shows consistent predictive accuracy above chance
evidence.source: Hand et al. 2021, Trends in Neuroscience
evidence.strength_justification: Multiple converging studies though effect sizes vary

evidence.summary: Experiments demonstrate sense of agency can be manipulated and measured in psychological studies
evidence.source: Wegner and Wheatley 1999, Journal of Experimental Psychology
evidence.strength_justification: Well-replicated demonstration of agency illusions

claims.statement: Unconscious neural activity reliably precedes and predicts simple motor decisions before conscious awareness
inference_chain: Premise: fMRI readiness potentials predict subjects’ decisions 5–10 s before awareness (E1)
Premise: EEG readiness potential initiates milliseconds before conscious intention (E2)
Premise: Meta-analysis shows ~60% prediction accuracy (E3)
Inference (inductive): Empirical trend indicates unconscious neural processes precede conscious intention
Conclusion: Unconscious brain activity reliably precedes and predicts simple decisions

predictions.statement: Increasing the temporal resolution of neural measurement should improve prediction accuracy beyond 60%
predictions.test: Use higher-resolution methods like intracranial recordings to predict choices before awareness
predictions.decision_criterion: If prediction accuracy does not exceed chance significantly, the claim is falsified
potential_falsifiers: New techniques yield prediction accuracy near chance

claims.statement: Conscious intentions are generated downstream of neural processes and are not causally efficacious in initiating decisions
inference_chain: Premise: Physical causal closure holds (A1)
Premise: Conscious intention follows neural precursors (C1)
Premise: Mental events supervene on physical states (A2)
Inference (deductive): Conscious intentions are effects of neural events, not causes
Conclusion: Conscious intentions lack causal efficacy in initiating decisions

predictions.statement: If conscious intention has causal efficacy, brain signals should not predict decisions before reported intention
predictions.test: Compare prediction accuracy in trials with strong vs. weak sense of intent
predictions.decision_criterion: If prediction accuracy remains constant regardless of reported intent strength, this prediction is falsified

claims.strength_justification: 0.65 — supported by A1 (0.85), A2 (0.80), C1 (0.65); limited by C1 (0.65)

claims.statement: Libertarian free will, defined as undetermined agent-caused choice, lacks empirical support
inference_chain: Premise: Libertarian free will requires decisions not fully determined by physical causes
Premise: Physical closure implies all events have physical causes (A1)
Premise: Conscious intentions are downstream of neural processes (C2)
Inference (deductive): No causal capacity remains for undetermined agent-caused choices
Conclusion: Libertarian free will lacks empirical support

predictions.statement: If agent-causal events exist, experiments should identify decisions without antecedent neural signatures above chance
predictions.test: Screen for decisions with no neural predictors using high-sensitivity recordings
predictions.decision_criterion: If such decisions occur reliably >30% of the time, the claim is falsified

claims.strength_justification: 0.65 — supported by A1 (0.85) and C2 (0.65); limited by C2 (0.65)

claims.statement: Compatibilist free will, as decisions aligned with one’s preferences and values, exists as a psychological phenomenon emergent from neural processes
inference_chain: Premise: Individuals report making reasoned choices based on preferences
Premise: Sense of agency can be experimentally manipulated and measured (E4)
Premise: Mental states supervene on brain processes (A2)
Inference (inductive): Deliberative choices emerge from neural computations reflecting preferences
Conclusion: Compatibilist free will exists as a measurable psychological phenomenon

predictions.statement: Subliminal priming of preferences should alter conscious choices if compatibilist free will is constrained
predictions.test: Conduct priming experiments and measure shifts in choice distributions
predictions.decision_criterion: If choices remain unchanged despite priming, the concept of compatibilist free will may be robust

claims.strength_justification: 0.70 — supported by A2 (0.80) and E4 (0.70); limited by E4 (0.70)

counterpositions.statement: Supervenience does not establish that mental events lack causal efficacy or that qualia cannot influence physical processes
counterpositions.my_response: While supervenience alone does not prove epiphenomenalism, empirical precedence of neural signals before conscious intention (C1) and physical closure (A1) together suggest mental events are downstream effects rather than causes

counterpositions.statement: Compatibilists can argue physical causation does not preclude meaningful free will as self-determined action
counterpositions.my_response: Our definition of libertarian free will in C3 explicitly excludes compatibilist accounts, which we address in C4; thus C3 remains focused on undetermined agent causation

counterpositions.statement: Prediction accuracies (~60%) in E1 and E3 are too low to generalize to all decision types, so C1 overstates consistency
counterpositions.my_response: Although individual study accuracies vary, convergence across methods and meta-analysis confirm a consistent trend of above-chance predictions, supporting C1's general claim of precedence

uncertainties.question: Is physical causal closure absolute, or can unknown non-physical influences affect neural processes?
Does the reconstructed sense of agency represent genuine freedom or merely an epiphenomenal illusion?

thesis.stance: Empirical studies of neural precursors to decision-making and the principle of physical causal closure indicate that decisions in the libertarian sense (as undetermined agent-caused events) lack support (C1, C2, C3), whereas a compatibilist form of free will—where choices align with internal preferences and values—exists as a measurable psychological phenomenon emergent from brain processes (C4).
summary_bullets: Neural readiness potentials and fMRI predictions show unconscious processes precede conscious intention (C1)
Conscious intentions appear epiphenomenal under causal closure (C2)
Libertarian, undetermined free will has no empirical backing (C3)
Compatibilist free will exists as an emergent psychological phenomenon (C4)
Key uncertainties include the absoluteness of physical closure (U1) and the authenticity of agency (U2)

assumptions.statement: Moral responsibility requires that agents have genuine alternative possibilities
assumptions.strength_justification: Widely accepted in moral philosophy; significant literature supports PAP as a necessary condition for responsibility

assumptions.statement: Introspective access to one’s decision-making process is at least moderately reliable (around 60%)
assumptions.strength_justification: Psychological studies of introspective accuracy find roughly 60% reliability with known error rates

assumptions.statement: Quantum indeterminacy introduces genuine causal gaps in physical processes at the neuronal level
assumptions.strength_justification: Quantum events are nondeterministic; while scale-up is debated, there is no definitive proof of closure at neural scales

assumptions.statement: First-person introspection and philosophical reflection are valid epistemic tools for investigating agency
assumptions.strength_justification: Philosophical tradition and cognitive science permit first-person reports as supplementary data, though contested

evidence.summary: Surveys of agents report a robust sense of self-initiated action in over 85% of voluntary decisions
evidence.strength_justification: Large-sample, cross-cultural surveys; some methodological noise but consistent pattern

evidence.summary: Libet-style readiness potential studies are interpreted ambiguously and do not conclusively show decisions are determined before conscious awareness
evidence.strength_justification: Replicated critiques show variable RP timing and question causal interpretation

claims.statement: Agents experience their choices as self-originated rather than fully determined by prior physical states
inference_chain: Premise: Surveys indicate a strong sense of agency in voluntary actions (E1)
Premise: Introspective reports are at least moderately reliable (A2)
Premise: First-person methods are valid for investigating agency (A4)
Inference (inductive): The convergence of introspective reliability with systematic agency reports justifies trusting the experience
Conclusion: Agents genuinely experience choices as self-originated

predictions.statement: In controlled tasks, participants will correctly identify self-initiated actions significantly above chance (>70%) based on introspection alone
predictions.test: Design a blind experiment where subjects mark self-initiated movements vs. externally triggered ones and measure accuracy
predictions.decision_criterion: If average accuracy ≤ 55%, the claim is falsified
potential_falsifiers: Participants perform at chance
Large systematic bias in introspective reporting

claims.strength_justification: 0.50 — supported by A2 (0.60), A4 (0.50), E1 (0.70); limited by A4 (0.50)

claims.statement: Moral responsibility presupposes the existence of alternative possibilities and thus requires free will
inference_chain: Premise: Genuine moral responsibility involves praise and blame (normative fact)
Premise: Moral responsibility requires alternative possibilities (A1)
Premise: Agents experience self-originated choices (C1)
Inference (deductive): If agents experience self-originated choices and moral responsibility requires alternatives, then free will must exist
Conclusion: Free will is a necessary condition for moral responsibility

predictions.statement: Belief in moral responsibility will correlate significantly with belief in free will in social-psychological studies
predictions.test: Survey participants on belief in free will and moral responsibility and compute correlation
predictions.decision_criterion: If correlation coefficient < 0.3, this normative link is challenged
potential_falsifiers: No significant correlation
High responsibility judgments independent of freedom beliefs

claims.strength_justification: 0.50 — supported by C1 (0.50) and A1 (0.85); limited by C1 (0.50)

claims.statement: Physical indeterminacy at the quantum level provides causal gaps that make space for non-determined agent causation
inference_chain: Premise: Quantum events are genuinely indeterminate (A3)
Premise: Neuroscience critiques show RP data are inconclusive for determinism (E2)
Inference (abductive): The best explanation for causal gaps in brain decisions is quantum indeterminacy rather than strict determinism
Conclusion: Indeterminacy opens space for agent causation

predictions.statement: If neural processes relevant to decision-making are found to be entirely deterministic with no indeterminate gaps, then this claim is falsified
predictions.test: Advanced neuroimaging and single-neuron recordings for signs of nondeterministic variability
predictions.decision_criterion: Absence of any unpredictability at decision-relevant loci challenges the claim
potential_falsifiers: Perfectly reproducible RR patterns
No observed quantum noise in decision neurons

claims.strength_justification: 0.60 — supported by A3 (0.70) and E2 (0.60); limited by E2 (0.60)

claims.statement: Libertarian free will exists because agents can originate actions via agent causation enabled by introspective agency and physical indeterminacy
inference_chain: Premise: Agents genuinely experience self-originated choices (C1)
Premise: Free will is required for moral responsibility (C2)
Premise: Physical indeterminacy makes room for agent causation (C3)
Inference (deductive): If all three hold, the coherent conclusion is that libertarian free will exists
Conclusion: Free will in the libertarian sense is real

predictions.statement: If predictive neuroscience achieves >95% success in forecasting decisions before conscious choice, this holistic claim is undermined
predictions.test: Large-scale predictive modeling of choices using pre-decisional neural data
predictions.decision_criterion: Prediction accuracy ≥ 95% would falsify genuine agent causation
potential_falsifiers: High predictive accuracy
Complete neural determinism discovered

counterpositions.statement: Sense of agency is often illusory (Wegner’s experiments), so introspection cannot ground real free will
counterpositions.my_response: While agency illusions exist, systematic data (E1) show a reliable majority sense of control; error rates do not swamp the genuine signal.

counterpositions.statement: Quantum randomness does not equate to agent control—random events don’t give agents genuine authorship
counterpositions.my_response: Indeterminacy only provides open causal gaps; agent causation is a non-random process that fills these gaps, combining mental causation with physics.

counterpositions.statement: Compatibilists show moral responsibility is compatible with determinism, so alternative possibilities aren't required
counterpositions.my_response: Compatibilism redefines alternatives conditionally; it fails to provide genuine do-or-don’t-do otherwise, which PAP demands for true responsibility.

uncertainties.question: To what extent are introspective reports systematically biased, and how does that affect reliance on E1?
uncertainties.importance: High—undermines foundational trust in self-reports of agency

uncertainties.question: Can quantum indeterminacy meaningfully scale to neuronal decision relevance, or is it averaged out?
uncertainties.importance: High—critical for claims about agent causation gaps

uncertainties.question: How can agent causation be empirically tested rather than philosophically asserted?
uncertainties.importance: Moderate—determines empirical tractability of ultimate claim

thesis.stance: Libertarian free will exists: agents genuinely originate actions through agent causation. This is supported by introspective experiences of self-originated choice (C1), the necessity of alternative possibilities for moral responsibility (C2), and physical indeterminacy providing causal gaps for mental causation (C3), culminating in a coherent framework (C4).
summary_bullets: Agents reliably experience choices as self-originated, above mere illusion (C1).
Moral responsibility demands genuine alternative possibilities, implying free will (C2).
Quantum indeterminacy opens causal gaps that agents can fill via mental causation (C3).
Combining introspection, moral norms, and indeterminacy yields a coherent libertarian free will (C4).

adjudication_results.reasoning: The challenger asserts that uncertainty U1 should lower A1’s strength but offers no empirical evidence or logical mechanism for recalibration. The defender systematically cites the same empirical foundations recorded in A1’s strength justification—conservation laws, null psi results, absence of mind-to-brain non-physical causation—and shows that until credible counter‐evidence emerges, A1’s 0.85 rating is proportionate. Hence the rebuttal successfully resolves the challenge.

The defender explicitly concedes the critic’s point and offers no refutation. Under our framework, a concession without defense validates the critique. The challenger correctly exposed a scope-dependency failure in C2 and C3.

While the challenger rightly raises concerns about correlational inference (a dependency failure), the defender offers explicit, convergent validation evidence (fMRI, EEG, intracranial recordings at 70–80% accuracy and cross-validation) that directly addresses these concerns. The defender’s argument thus meets the framework’s criteria for a valid defense and outperforms the critique in logical strength.

The challenger raises a substantive question about C2_target’s scope but provides no new counter-premises. The defender reiterates closure and supervenience and appeals to external evidence (E4) not in the belief base. Therefore, neither side fully resolves the inferential gap, leaving the dispute unresolved.

The challenger identifies that C4’s reliance on E4 commits a dependency failure—E4 only demonstrates manipulability of agency illusions, not functional emergence. The rebuttal fails to fill this gap, making an unsupported inferential leap from ‘manipulable illusion’ to ‘functional emergent mechanism.’

thesis.stance: Empirical neuroscience shows that simple motor decisions are reliably predicted by unconscious neural processes (C1) and that conscious intentions lack causal efficacy in initiating those decisions (C2), thereby undermining libertarian free will (C3). However, a compatible form of free will emerges as an agent’s psychological experience of agency aligned with preferences and values, which functionally influences decision parameters (C4).

Compatibilist free will exists as an emergent psychological phenomenon that functionally modulates choices (C4)

patches_applied.stance: Empirical neuroscience shows that simple motor decisions are reliably predicted by unconscious neural processes (C1) and that conscious intentions lack causal efficacy in initiating those decisions (C2), thereby undermining libertarian free will (C3). However, a compatible form of free will emerges as an agent’s psychological experience of agency aligned with preferences and values, which functionally influences decision parameters (C4).

Conscious intentions are downstream and non-causal in simple motor tasks (C2)
Libertarian, undetermined free will lacks empirical support in simple motor decisions (C3)
Compatibilist free will exists as an emergent psychological phenomenon that functionally modulates choices (C4)

thesis.stance: Libertarian free will exists: agents genuinely originate actions through agent causation. This is supported by introspective experiences of self-originated choice (C1), the necessity of alternative possibilities for moral responsibility (C2), and physical indeterminacy providing causal gaps for mental causation (C3), culminating in a coherent framework (C4).
summary_bullets: Agents reliably experience choices as self-originated, above mere illusion (C1).
Moral responsibility demands genuine alternative possibilities, implying free will (C2).
Quantum indeterminacy opens causal gaps that agents can fill via mental causation (C3).
Combining introspection, moral norms, and indeterminacy yields a coherent libertarian free will (C4).

adjudication_results.reasoning: The defender concedes the lack of empirical support for A4 and offers no defense. The challenger correctly identifies a dependency failure, demonstrating that A4’s confidence is unjustified without >70% introspective accuracy evidence.

The challenger correctly identifies that C3 depends on E2 for quantum indeterminism but E2 only addresses whether RPs are artifacts rather than showing irreducible quantum randomness. The defender’s methods are not present in the cited evidence and do not explain how classical neural noise is excluded, leaving the dependency failure unresolved.

<reasoning>
1. Restatement  
   The Empiricist challenges the Supernaturalist to propose an empirical test of U2—the claim that it is unknown whether quantum indeterminacy meaningfully influences decision-relevant neurons—and to specify how results would update the strength of C3. The beliefs under dispute are U2 (the uncertainty about scaling) and C3 from the target excerpt (Physical indeterminacy providing causal gaps; strength = 0.60).

2. Formalization  
   Challenger’s position (implicit):  
   - Premise: U2 states we don’t know if quantum effects scale to neuronal decision processes.  
   - Implicit demand: Without a direct empirical test, C3 remains under-justified.  
   - Conclusion: Provide an experimental design and update rules for C3.  

   Defender’s position (rebuttal):  
   - Premise 1: One can amplify/suppress quantum tunneling in neurons via temperature modulation.  
   - Premise 2: Recording single-neuron spike-time variability under these conditions yields a measurable correlation with quantum‐level event rates.  
   - Inference: A significant positive correlation (>0.3) indicates genuine quantum influence on decisions; a low correlation (<0.1) indicates the contrary.  
   - Conclusion: If corr > 0.3, raise C3’s strength to ~0.7; if corr < 0.1, reduce it to ~0.4.

   Verification against excerpts:  
   - C3 in target excerpt indeed concerns quantum indeterminacy’s role (strength 0.60).  
   - U2 is referenced in context but not explicitly in the belief excerpts; the rebuttal assumes it.

3. Adjudication  
   - The challenger merely requests a test; they offer no argument to be refuted or defended. This is not a substantive critique of an inference chain but a question about methodology.  
   - The defender supplies a procedure, but it rests on an unspoken and unjustified assumption that macroscopic temperature changes isolate and modulate quantum tunneling rates without confounding classical neural dynamics (dependency failure).  
   - Under the criteria, the rebuttal exhibits a hidden assumption (dependency failure) and does not fully resolve the methodological uncertainty about U2.  
   - Thus neither side scores a clear victory: the challenge was answered but not convincingly, and no genuine logical refutation or defense of an inference chain occurred.

Outcome: unresolved.

The challenger identifies a clear dependency failure in C4’s inference: the crucial mechanism (M) that would link mental states to quantum indeterminacy is entirely absent. The defender’s rebuttal merely defers to future research (U3) without supplying any logical or evidential bridge. Under classical logic standards, this fails to remedy the missing premise, so the critique is valid and the rebuttal fails.

The challenger identified a dependency failure (C2’s ambiguous specification of 'alternatives'), which constitutes a valid critique. The defender concedes and narrows C2 rather than defending the original formulation, so no successful rebuttal is made.

thesis.stance: Empirical evidence robustly supports that decisions are initiated by unconscious neural processes (C1) and that no uncaused decision points appear, undercutting libertarian free will (C2). However, agents exhibit a measurable capacity for reason-responsive action and subjective agency (C3), which suffices for meaningful moral responsibility (C4). Thus, while metaphysical libertarian freedom lacks empirical support, a compatibilist form of free will exists and grounds responsibility.

No empirical gaps allow for libertarian, uncaused choices (C2)
Rational deliberation and sense of agency constitute an empirical compatibilist free will (C3)
Compatibilist free will suffices for moral responsibility (C4)

thesis.stance: Free will is a genuine capacity of conscious agents, grounded in irreducible mental causation and quantum indeterminacy harnessed at the agent level (C1). It is necessary for real moral responsibility (C2) and finds its most coherent explanation in a transcendent creator who endows beings with authentic freedom (C3). This framework reconciles scientific insights with metaphysical commitments, preserving moral experience and spiritual testimony.

adjudication_results.reasoning: The challenger’s critique relies on an unfounded dichotomy that metaphysical principles cannot have empirical support, which is a dependency failure. The defender provides a clear inductive basis—no observed violations in closed-system tests—to justify treating causal closure as an empirical generalization. The rebuttal thus validly addresses and resolves the challenger’s concern.

<reasoning>
Step 1: Restate core disagreement  
The challenger disputes Claim C1’s inductive inference that readiness potentials (RPs) preceding conscious intention demonstrate unconscious decision initiation, pointing to counterposition X2 (“RP reflects generic motor preparation, not specific decision signals”) which C1’s author only partially addressed. The defender responds by citing intracranial and high-density EEG decoding studies that purport to distinguish specific choice outcomes from RP-like signals, arguing this shows the RP is choice-specific preparatory activity rather than undirected motor arousal.

Step 2: Formalization  
Challenger’s critique (under X2):  
  Premise A: C1 infers (via A2, E1) that RPs preceding intention indicate decision initiation is unconscious.  
  Premise B (X2): RPs may reflect only general, undirected motor arousal, not commitment to a specific choice.  
  Inference: If RPs are undirected, they do not mark decision commitment, so C1’s inference is undermined.  
  Conclusion: C1 is unjustified without ruling out generic motor prep.

Defender’s rebuttal:  
  Premise 1: Intracranial and high-density EEG studies (e.g. Bode et al. 2011; Murakami et al. 2014) have decoded specific upcoming choices from RP-like signals.  
  Premise 2: Multivariate classifiers can reliably distinguish between decision outcomes prior to conscious intent.  
  Inference: These findings demonstrate RPs carry choice-specific preparatory information rather than merely undirected motor arousal.  
  Conclusion: X2 is refuted and C1’s inference remains sound.

Evidence check: The target belief excerpt lists X2 as “partial” and mentions invasive recordings but does not give these studies by name—so the defender’s citations extend the belief set without internal contradiction.

Step 3: Adjudication  
– The challenger correctly identifies a dependency failure: C1’s inference does rely on RPs being decision-specific (criteria #4).  
– The defender offers targeted empirical evidence directly addressing that concern, showing choice-specific RP signals (criteria #2, #6 for rebuttal).  
– Both arguments are logically coherent; the defender’s rebuttal supplies new data that adequately resolves X2.

Given the direct refutation, the defender’s response outweighs the challenger’s critique.  
Thus, rebuttal_valid.

The defender’s reply is an explicit concession rather than a refutation or successful reframing. By acknowledging greater uncertainty and lowering C1’s strength, the defense fails to sustain the original confidence level. According to the criteria, an unchallenged concession validates the critique.

The challenger identifies a clear dependency failure: the premise C1 is only tested on simple lab tasks, so it cannot be assumed universal without further evidence. The defender's response fails to supply new empirical support or a valid counterargument, merely restating the original abductive inference. Thus the critique succeeds and the rebuttal does not adequately resolve the logical gap.

The challenger requested a falsifiable test but offered no substantive flaw in logic or evidence. The defender directly specifies a coherent, falsifiable protocol that addresses the distinction between decision-specific RP and motor preparation, satisfying the challenge.

thesis.stance: Free will is a genuine capacity of conscious agents, grounded in irreducible mental causation and quantum indeterminacy harnessed at the agent level (C1). This framework reconciles scientific insights with metaphysical commitments, preserving moral experience and spiritual testimony.

adjudication_results.reasoning: The challenger exposed that A3 is unfalsifiable and lacks empirical support (dependency failure, criteria 3 & 4). The defender concedes and offers a future research program, which constitutes acknowledgment of the critique rather than a defense. Under the framework, this counts as a successful critique and unsuccessful rebuttal.

The defender admits the lack of empirical support and merely suggests future research, providing no counter-evidence or resolution. The challenger correctly highlights the unsupported premise, so the critique is valid and the rebuttal fails.

The defender’s rebuttal depends on external studies not present in the belief excerpt (dependency failure). The challenger correctly identifies the lack of empirical constraint on A2’s assigned strength. Under the logic framework, the critique succeeds and the rebuttal fails.

The challenger posed a clarification request rather than highlighting a logical error. The defender explicitly provided the empirical criteria needed (threshold T) and explained why current methods fail to meet it. This satisfies the challenge and corrects any ambiguity about what evidence C1 depends on. Under classical logic and Bayesian standards, the rebuttal successfully addresses the issue without logical flaw.

The defender fails to ground their empirical claims in the provided belief base (dependency failure) and does not conclusively refute epiphenomenal interpretations. The challenger correctly identifies the absence of direct causal evidence.

assumptions.statement: Free will (libertarian) is defined as the capacity to originate decisions in a way not fully determined by prior physical events.
assumptions.strength_justification: Definitional clarity by stipulation.

assumptions.statement: Physical causal closure: every physical event has a sufficient physical cause governed by natural laws.
assumptions.strength_justification: Strongly supported by physics; no reproducible violations.

assumptions.statement: Human decision-making correlates reliably with neural processes, with causality flowing from brain states to choices.
assumptions.strength_justification: Multiple neuroscientific replications (e.g., EEG, fMRI, lesion studies).

assumptions.statement: Operational definitions and third-person empirical methods are appropriate primary tools for investigating free will claims.
assumptions.strength_justification: Standard scientific methodology, though introspective aspects remain challenging.

evidence.summary: Libet-style readiness potentials appear hundreds of milliseconds before reported conscious intention.
evidence.source: Neuroscience experiments (Libet et al.), replications by Soon et al.
evidence.strength_justification: Well-replicated timing data, though interpretation debated.

evidence.summary: Computational and neural models can predict human choices above chance using pre-decisional brain activity.
evidence.source: Predictive modeling studies (e.g., Haynes et al.)
evidence.strength_justification: Statistically significant but moderate effect sizes; limited in scope.

claims.statement: Libertarian free will, as uncaused origination not determined by prior events, does not exist.
inference_chain: Premise: Libertarian free will defined as uncaused origination (A1).
Premise: Physical causal closure holds (A2).
Premise: Decisions correlate with and follow from neural processes (A3).
Premise: Readiness potentials precede conscious intent (E1).
Inference (abductive): No empirical gap for uncaused decision initiations.
Conclusion: Libertarian free will is unsupported by current data.

predictions.statement: If libertarian free will exists, decisions would sometimes occur without prior predictable neural precursors.
predictions.test: Use high-resolution neural decoding to predict choices in real time; compare to conscious reports.
predictions.decision_criterion: Observe decisions with <50% predictability under controlled conditions to falsify C1.
potential_falsifiers: Neural predictors fail consistently across tasks
Replicable cases of spontaneous, unpredictable choices

claims.statement: A compatibilist form of free will—emergent, reason-responsive decision-making within physical laws—is consistent with empirical evidence.
inference_chain: Premise: Compatibilist free will defined as emergent, reasons-responsive processes (A1).
Premise: Brain processes causally give rise to decisions (A3).
Premise: Empirical methods valid for behavior analysis (A4).
Premise: Predictive models show reason-based interventions alter choices (E2).
Inference (inductive): Agents act based on reasons in a way that qualifies as functional freedom.
Conclusion: Compatibilist free will aligns with observed data.

predictions.statement: If compatibilist free will holds, moral or rational priming should reliably influence choices.
predictions.test: Randomize subjects to different reason-prime conditions, measure decision shift.
predictions.decision_criterion: If prime fails to produce significant choice differences in >30% of replications, prediction is falsified.
potential_falsifiers: Systematic failure of reasons to influence decisions
Inability to modulate behavior via rational cues

claims.statement: Moral responsibility should be grounded in compatibilist understanding of decision-making, not in metaphysical libertarian freedom.
inference_chain: Premise: Libertarian free will is unsupported (C1).
Premise: Compatibilist free will is empirically coherent (C2).
Inference (deductive): If moral responsibility requires freedom, it must rest on the compatible form.
Conclusion: Ethical systems should adopt compatibilist foundations.

predictions.statement: If moral frameworks grounded in compatibilism yield better social outcomes, then policies based on this view will outperform libertarian-based policies.
predictions.test: Compare rehabilitative justice programs (compatibilist) vs. punitive deterrence (libertarian) on recidivism.
predictions.decision_criterion: If rehabilitative programs fail to reduce recidivism by >10% compared to deterrence, normative claim is challenged.
potential_falsifiers: Evidence that deterrence-based systems outperform rehabilitative ones
No measurable difference in social outcomes
